fuzzer_role.value_profile: primary pair A — has CMP_MAP gradient

expected_direction: vp >> naive at every DERIVATION_DEPTH; vp/naive ratio approximately FLAT (depth-invariance is the load-bearing claim)

expected_curve_per_fuzzer.value_profile: approximately flat across DERIVATION_DEPTH IF the 64-bit gradient is wide enough to bootstrap at depth=0
expected_curve_per_fuzzer.naive: ≈ 0 at every dose; 1/2^64 per attempt regardless of depth

acceptance.rule: vp median > 10x naive median at depth=0 AND vp_rate at depth ∈ {1,2,4} within 2x of vp_rate at depth=0

design_basis: Single 64-bit equality CMP at the trap; DERIVATION_DEPTH bijective XOR-with-constant layers between the input read and the CMP. Each layer is a noinline function-pointer-table call writing to a volatile sink (prevents constant-folding the chain into a single XOR). Hamming distance between layered output and TRAP_TARGET equals Hamming distance between raw input and (TRAP_TARGET XOR cumulative_mask). VP's gradient over input bytes is isomorphic to its gradient at the CMP under any depth; if VP's gradient bootstraps at depth=0, depth-invariance should hold.
notes: DERIVATION_DEPTH is COMPILE-TIME; one Dockerfile per scan value at libafl_fuzzbench/docker/targets/Dockerfile.b_vp_deriv_v3_d{0,1,2,4}.
Initial seed: 8 bytes of zero. With XOR_MASKS chosen such that none equal the trap target's pre-image, the seed is GUARANTEED non-trap at every depth — no spurious crash on first execution.

If vp ≈ naive at depth=0, the verdict is 'refuted_for_single_cmp_gradient_narrowness' — does NOT refute the bijective-derivation feature itself; refutes only this synthesis design's ability to test it. In that case the next experiment is a wider-operand variant (e.g. 128-bit equality split into two cmps, accepting that this slightly compromises 'one cmp' fidelity).

If vp >> naive at depth=0 AND ratio degrades monotonically with depth, the verdict is 'refuted' or 'partially_reproduced' — the bijective transform DOES degrade the gradient, contradicting the load-bearing depth-invariance claim. This would be a substantive finding correcting the original feature description.

Compared to v2 (chain-compounding gradient): v2's mechanism is gradient-compounding-across-K-CMPs; v3's mechanism is gradient-preservation-through-bijective-derivation. These are STRUCTURALLY DIFFERENT features; v2 should be promoted to its own template, v3 returns this template to its original feature claim.
